```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Delia"; "Hope"; "Patricia"; "Wayne").
price(5; 6; 7; 8).
drink("cafe americano"; "cafe latte"; "cappuccino"; "chai tea").

% For each name, there is exactly one drink and one price associated.
{order(Name, Drink, Price): drink(Drink), price(Price)}=1 :- names(Name).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
:- order(N1,D1,P1), order(N2,D2,P2), N1 != N2, (D1 == D2 ; P1 == P2).
:- order(N1,D1,P1), order(N2,D2,P2), D1 != D2, P1 != P2, N1 != N2, (N1 == N2 ; D1 == D2 ; P1 == P2).

% 1. Hope paid less than Delia.
:- order("Hope", P1, _), order("Delia", P2, _), P1 >= P2.

% 2. Patricia had the cappuccino.
:- order(N, D, P), N = "Patricia", D != "cappuccino".

% 3. The one who had the cafe americano paid 1 dollar more than Patricia.
:- order(N1, "cafe americano", P1), order("Patricia", "cappuccino", P2), P1 != P2 + 1.

% 4. Hope paid more than the one who had the cafe americano.
:- order("Hope", P1, _), order(N, "cafe americano", P2), P1 <= P2.

% 5. The one who had the chai tea paid 1 dollar more than the one who had the cafe americano.
:- order(N1, "chai tea", P1), order(N2, "cafe americano", P2), P1 != P2 + 1.

% Ensure all values are uniquely assigned: one-to-one mapping between names, drinks, and prices.
% Enforce bijective constraints explicitly to eliminate ambiguity.
:- {order(N, D, P) : price(P)} != 1, names(N).
:- {order(N, D, P) : drink(D)} != 1, names(N).
:- {order(N, D, P) : names(N)} != 1, drink(D).
:- {order(N, D, P) : names(N)} != 1, price(P).
:- {order(N, D, P) : drink(D)} != 1, price(P).
:- {order(N, D, P) : price(P)} != 1, drink(D).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
diplomat("Fitzgerald"; "Howell"; "Riggs"; "Vasquez").
month(1; 2; 3; 4).
duration(2; 6; 8; 9).

% For each diplomat D, assign exactly 1 month M and 1 duration T.
{assign(D, M, T): month(M), duration(T)}=1 :- diplomat(D).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
:- assign(D1,M1,T1), assign(D2,M2,T2), D1 != D2, M1 == M2.
:- assign(D1,M1,T1), assign(D2,M2,T2), D1 != D2, T1 == T2.
:- assign(D1,M1,T1), assign(D2,M2,T2), D1 != D2, M1 == M2, T1 == T2.

% 1. Howell will leave sometime after Fitzgerald.
:- assign("Howell", M1, _), assign("Fitzgerald", M2, _), M1 <= M2.

% 2. Howell will leave sometime before the ambassador with the 6 day visit.
:- assign("Howell", M1, _), assign(D, M2, 6), M1 >= M2.

% 3. Vasquez will leave 1 month before the ambassador with the 2 day visit.
:- assign("Vasquez", M1, _), assign(D, M2, 2), M1 != M2 - 1.

% 4. The ambassador with the 8 day visit will leave 2 months before Vasquez.
:- assign(D, M1, 8), assign("Vasquez", M2, _), M1 != M2 - 2.

% Additional integrity constraint: ensure all months and durations are used exactly once.
% This is enforced via the uniqueness constraints above, but we add explicit cardinality to prevent over-assignment.
:- #count{M: month(M)} != 4.
:- #count{T: duration(T)} != 4.
:- #count{D: diplomat(D)} != 4.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun").
year(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_color("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

% For each name N, it matches with exactly 1 year Y and 1 suit color S.
{match(N, Y, S) : year(Y), suit_color(S)} = 1 :- names(N).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
:- #count{N, Y, S : match(N, Y, S)} != 4.
:- #count{N, Y, S : match(N, Y, S), N=N1} > 1 :- names(N1).
:- #count{N, Y, S : match(N, Y, S), Y=Y1} > 1 :- year(Y1).
:- #count{N, Y, S : match(N, Y, S), S=S1} > 1 :- suit_color(S1).

% 1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
:- match("Jorge", Y, S), S != "red", Y != 1982.

% 2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
:- match(N1, Y1, "pink"), match("Otis", Y2, _), Y1 != Y2 - 1.

% 3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
:- match(N1, Y1, "yellow"), match("Philip", Y2, _), Y1 <= Y2.

% 4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
:- match(N1, Y1, "lime green"), match(N2, Y2, "yellow"), Y1 <= Y2.

% 5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.
:- match(N1, Y1, "pink"), match("Shaun", Y2, _), Y1 != Y2 - 2.

% Ensure all variables are uniquely assigned via explicit uniqueness constraints.
% This ensures that each name, year, and suit is used exactly once.
:- #count{N : match(N, Y, S)} != 1 :- year(Y), suit_color(S).
:- #count{Y : match(N, Y, S)} != 1 :- names(N), suit_color(S).
:- #count{S : match(N, Y, S)} != 1 :- names(N), year(Y).
```

pieces(250; 500; 750; 1000).
themes("autumn leaves"; "coral reef"; "outer space"; "rustic village").

% For each company C, it matches with exactly 1 piece P and 1 theme T.
{puzzle(C, P, T): pieces(P), themes(T)}=1 :- companies(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
:- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1 != C2, P1 == P2.
:- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1 != C2, T1 == T2.

% Ensure that all four distinct entities in constraint 2 are present and distinct
% The four puzzles are: Buralde, rustic village, autumn leaves, 1000 pieces
% So each of these must appear exactly once, and they must refer to distinct puzzles
% We enforce that Buralde, rustic village, autumn leaves, and 1000 pieces are all represented
:- not puzzle("Buralde", _, _).
:- not puzzle(_, P, "rustic village").
:- not puzzle(_, P, "autumn leaves").
:- not puzzle(_, 1000, _).

% The jigsaw puzzle made by Buralde has the outer space theme.
:- puzzle("Buralde", _, T), T != "outer space".

% The puzzle with the autumn leaves theme has more pieces than the rustic village theme.
:- puzzle(_, P1, "autumn leaves"), puzzle(_, P2, "rustic village"), P1 <= P2.

% The jigsaw puzzle made by Denlend has fewer pieces than the puzzle with the outer space theme.
:- puzzle("Denlend", P1, _), puzzle(_, P2, "outer space"), P1 >= P2.

% The jigsaw puzzle made by Kimsight has exactly 250 more pieces than the puzzle made by Denlend.
:- puzzle("Kimsight", P1, _), puzzle("Denlend", P2, _), P1 != P2 + 250.

% Additional integrity constraints to eliminate ambiguity:
% 1. The outer space theme puzzle must be made by Buralde (from constraint 3), so no other company can have outer space.
:- puzzle(C, _, "outer space"), C != "Buralde".

% 2. The puzzle with 1000 pieces must be one of the four, and we already enforce it's present.
%    But to avoid multiple possibilities, we can deduce that since Buralde has outer space, and
%    the puzzle with 1000 pieces is one of the four, we must ensure consistency.

% - Kimsight = Denlend + 250

companies("Omnipax"; "Rubicorp"; "SpaceZen"; "Ubersplore").
rockets("Beritron"; "Exatris"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Each company launches exactly one rocket in exactly one month.
{launches(C, R, M) : rockets(R), months(M)} = 1 :- companies(C).

% Each rocket is launched by exactly one company in exactly one month.
{launches(C, R, M) : companies(C), months(M)} = 1 :- rockets(R).

% Each month has exactly one launch.
{launches(C, R, M) : companies(C), rockets(R)} = 1 :- months(M).

% Ensure that if Worul launches in month M, Exatris launches in M+1.
% Use a constraint to forbid any violation.
:- launches(C1, "Worul", M), launches(C2, "Exatris", M2), M2 != M + 1.

% 2.1 The Foltron and the rocket that will launch in January are different.
% So the rocket launched in January is not Foltron.
:- launches(C, "Foltron", 1).

% 2.2 The Foltron is made by Ubersplore or made by Rubicorp.
% So the company developing Foltron must be Ubersplore or Rubicorp.
:- launches(C, "Foltron", M), C != "Ubersplore", C != "Rubicorp".

% 2.3 The rocket that will launch in January is made by Ubersplore or made by Rubicorp.
% So the company launching in January must be Ubersplore or Rubicorp.
:- launches(C, R, 1), C != "Ubersplore", C != "Rubicorp".

% 3. The rocket developed by SpaceZen will launch 2 months after the rocket developed by Ubersplore.
% So SpaceZen launches at M1, Ubersplore at M2, and M1 = M2 + 2.
:- launches("SpaceZen", R1, M1), launches("Ubersplore", R2, M2), M1 != M2 + 2.

% 4. The rocket that will launch in February is either the Worul or the rocket developed by Omnipax.
% So: if launched in February, then either the rocket is Worul or the company is Omnipax.
:- launches(C, R, 2), R != "Worul", C != "Omnipax".

capacities(110; 150; 190; 230).
sports("basketball"; "football"; "lacrosse"; "soccer").

{arena(A, C, S) : capacities(C), sports(S)} = 1 :- arenas(A).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
:- arena(A1, C1, S1), arena(A2, C2, S2), A1 != A2, C1 = C2, S1 = S2.
:- arena(A1, C1, S1), arena(A2, C2, S2), A1 != A2, C1 = C2.
:- arena(A1, C1, S1), arena(A2, C2, S2), A1 != A2, S1 = S2.

:- arena(A1, C1, "football"), arena(A2, C2, _), A2 = "Underwood", C1 <= C2.

:- arena(A1, C1, "basketball"), arena(A2, C2, _), A2 = "Vazquez", C1 != C2 + 80.

:- arena(A, C, S), A = "Young", C != 230.

% 4. Underwood Arena holds 40 fewer people than Vazquez Arena.
:- arena("Underwood", C1, _), arena("Vazquez", C2, _), C1 != C2 - 40.
